title: Hypoglycemia
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Emergency Medicine, Endocrinology, Internal Medicine
objectives: Recognize Whipple's triad and hypoglycemia symptoms
Differentiate causes in diabetic vs non-diabetic patients
Apply systematic workup for non-diabetic hypoglycemia

vignette.text:
A 45-year-old woman is brought to the ED by EMS after being found confused at work. Coworkers report she was diaphoretic and tremulous before becoming unresponsive. Fingerstick glucose by EMS was 38 mg/dL. She improved after IV dextrose.

Past Medical History: No diabetes, no prior hypoglycemia

Medications: None

Allergies: NKDA

Social: Works as accountant, no alcohol use

physicalExam.neuro: Now alert and oriented, no focal deficits

questions.stem: This patient presented with confusion and glucose of 38 mg/dL.
questions.question: What defines hypoglycemia and how does it present?

options.text: Autonomic symptoms occur after neuroglycopenic
Whipple's triad requires diabetes diagnosis

WHIPPLE'S TRIAD (defines true hypoglycemia): (1) Symptoms consistent with hypoglycemia; (2) Low plasma glucose at time of symptoms; (3) Resolution of symptoms with glucose correction; SYMPTOMS: AUTONOMIC (adrenergic): Tremor, palpitations, anxiety; Diaphoresis, hunger; Pallor, tachycardia; NEUROGLYCOPENIC: Confusion, difficulty concentrating; Behavioral changes, irritability; Visual changes, slurred speech; Weakness, fatigue; Seizures, loss of consciousness; GLUCOSE THRESHOLDS: <70 mg/dL: Alert threshold (counter-regulatory hormones); <54 mg/dL: Clinically significant hypoglycemia; <40 mg/dL: Severe, neuroglycopenia common; This patient: All 3 criteria met - symptoms + glucose 38 + resolution with treatment
Hypoglycemia is defined as glucose <100 mg/dL

questions.explanation: Hypoglycemia Recognition: WHIPPLE'S TRIAD is required to diagnose true hypoglycemia: (1) Symptoms consistent with hypoglycemia, (2) Documented low glucose at time of symptoms, (3) Resolution with glucose treatment. SYMPTOMS by type: AUTONOMIC (adrenergic response): Tremor, palpitations, diaphoresis, anxiety, hunger, pallor. NEUROGLYCOPENIC (brain glucose deprivation): Confusion, behavioral changes, visual disturbances, weakness, seizures, LOC. GLUCOSE THRESHOLDS: <70 mg/dL activates counter-regulatory response. <54 mg/dL is clinically significant. <40 mg/dL typically causes neuroglycopenic symptoms.

questions.question: What causes hypoglycemia in diabetic patients?

options.text: DIABETIC HYPOGLYCEMIA CAUSES: INSULIN: Excessive dose; Timing mismatch with meals; Injection site issues (lipohypertrophy); Exercise increasing absorption; SULFONYLUREAS: Long-acting (glipizide, glyburide, glimepiride); Especially with renal impairment; Accumulation in elderly; REDUCED GLUCOSE PRODUCTION: Alcohol (inhibits gluconeogenesis); Hepatic dysfunction; INCREASED GLUCOSE UTILIZATION: Unplanned exercise; Missed meals; IMPAIRED COUNTER-REGULATION: Hypoglycemia unawareness; Long-standing diabetes; Prior hypoglycemic episodes; Autonomic neuropathy; RISK FACTORS: Renal insufficiency; Hepatic disease; Advanced age; Tight glycemic targets; Duration of diabetes
Metformin commonly causes hypoglycemia
Exercise decreases hypoglycemia risk
Only insulin causes hypoglycemia in diabetes

questions.explanation: Diabetic Hypoglycemia Causes: MEDICATIONS: Insulin (dose, timing, absorption issues), Sulfonylureas (especially long-acting, accumulate in renal failure). Note: Metformin alone does NOT cause hypoglycemia. REDUCED GLUCOSE PRODUCTION: Alcohol inhibits gluconeogenesis, hepatic dysfunction. INCREASED UTILIZATION: Exercise, missed meals. IMPAIRED COUNTER-REGULATION: Hypoglycemia unawareness develops with recurrent hypoglycemia, autonomic neuropathy. RISK FACTORS: Renal/hepatic impairment, elderly, tight targets, long diabetes duration.

questions.stem: This patient has no diabetes. Her hypoglycemia requires explanation.
questions.question: What causes hypoglycemia in non-diabetic patients?

options.text: NON-DIABETIC HYPOGLYCEMIA CAUSES: MEDICATIONS/TOXINS: Sulfonylureas (accidental/surreptitious); Insulin (factitious); Alcohol; Beta-blockers (mask symptoms); Quinolones, pentamidine; ENDOGENOUS HYPERINSULINISM: Insulinoma (most common tumor cause); Nesidioblastosis; Insulin autoimmune syndrome (rare); ORGAN FAILURE: Hepatic failure (↓gluconeogenesis); Renal failure (↓insulin clearance); Sepsis; Adrenal insufficiency; HORMONAL: Cortisol deficiency; Growth hormone deficiency; Glucagon deficiency (rare); NON-ISLET CELL TUMORS: Large mesenchymal tumors; IGF-2 secretion; POST-GASTRIC SURGERY: Dumping syndrome; Post-bariatric reactive hypoglycemia; This patient: Needs workup for insulinoma vs factitious vs other
Non-diabetic hypoglycemia is always factitious
Adrenal insufficiency doesn't cause hypoglycemia
Insulinoma is the most common cause

questions.explanation: Non-Diabetic Hypoglycemia Causes: MEDICATIONS/TOXINS: Sulfonylureas (accidental or surreptitious), insulin (factitious), alcohol, quinolones, pentamidine. ENDOGENOUS HYPERINSULINISM: Insulinoma (rare but important), nesidioblastosis, insulin autoimmune syndrome. ORGAN FAILURE: Hepatic (decreased gluconeogenesis), renal (decreased insulin clearance), sepsis. HORMONAL: Adrenal insufficiency, cortisol deficiency. NON-ISLET TUMORS: Large tumors secreting IGF-2. POST-GASTRIC SURGERY: Reactive hypoglycemia after bariatric surgery.

questions.stem: You need to determine the cause of her hypoglycemia.
questions.question: What is the diagnostic workup for non-diabetic hypoglycemia?

options.text: Workup can be done when glucose is normal
C-peptide is high with exogenous insulin

CRITICAL SAMPLE (during hypoglycemia): Plasma glucose (confirm); Insulin; C-peptide; Proinsulin; Beta-hydroxybutyrate; Sulfonylurea/meglitinide screen; Insulin antibodies (if available); INTERPRETATION: HIGH insulin + HIGH C-peptide: Endogenous hyperinsulinism (insulinoma, sulfonylurea, nesidioblastosis); HIGH insulin + LOW C-peptide: Exogenous insulin (factitious); LOW insulin + LOW C-peptide: Non-insulin mediated (organ failure, IGF-2 tumor, cortisol deficiency); Beta-hydroxybutyrate LOW: Suggests insulin-mediated; Beta-hydroxybutyrate HIGH: Suggests non-insulin cause; ADDITIONAL: Cortisol level (if suspect adrenal); IGF-2:IGF-1 ratio (if suspect tumor); Imaging only AFTER biochemical confirmation
Imaging is the first step in workup

questions.explanation: Hypoglycemia Diagnostic Workup: CRITICAL SAMPLE during hypoglycemia (<55 mg/dL): Glucose, insulin, C-peptide, proinsulin, beta-hydroxybutyrate, sulfonylurea screen. INTERPRETATION: HIGH insulin + HIGH C-peptide = endogenous source (insulinoma, sulfonylurea). HIGH insulin + LOW C-peptide = exogenous insulin (factitious). LOW insulin + LOW C-peptide = non-insulin mediated (organ failure, cortisol deficiency). Beta-hydroxybutyrate suppressed = insulin-mediated. IMAGING only after biochemical confirmation of endogenous hyperinsulinism.

questions.stem: Critical labs show: Glucose 42, Insulin 18 µU/mL (high), C-peptide 3.2 ng/mL (high), sulfonylurea screen negative.
questions.question: What do these results indicate?

options.text: INTERPRETATION OF RESULTS: High insulin + High C-peptide + Negative sulfonylurea = ENDOGENOUS HYPERINSULINISM; INSULINOMA criteria (all must be present): Glucose ≤55 mg/dL with symptoms; Insulin ≥3 µU/mL (inappropriately elevated); C-peptide ≥0.6 ng/mL; Proinsulin ≥5.0 pmol/L; Beta-hydroxybutyrate ≤2.7 mmol/L; Negative sulfonylurea screen; This patient meets criteria; NEXT STEPS: CT or MRI pancreas (detects 70-80%); If negative: Endoscopic ultrasound (most sensitive 90-95%); If still negative: Selective arterial calcium stimulation test; NOTE: Do NOT image before biochemical diagnosis - incidentalomas common
These results indicate exogenous insulin use
Sulfonylurea ingestion is likely
No further workup needed

questions.explanation: Results Interpretation: High insulin + High C-peptide + Negative sulfonylurea screen = ENDOGENOUS HYPERINSULINISM, most likely INSULINOMA. INSULINOMA DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA: All must be present during hypoglycemia - glucose ≤55, insulin ≥3, C-peptide ≥0.6, negative sulfonylurea screen. LOCALIZATION: CT/MRI pancreas first (70-80% sensitivity). EUS if negative (90-95% sensitivity). Selective arterial calcium stimulation if still occult. IMPORTANT: Do not image before biochemical diagnosis as pancreatic incidentalomas are common.

questions.stem: A patient presents with hypoglycemia and altered mental status.
questions.question: How should acute hypoglycemia be managed?

options.text: D50W is preferred for sulfonylurea hypoglycemia
One dose of glucose always sufficient
Glucagon works immediately

ACUTE HYPOGLYCEMIA MANAGEMENT: IF CONSCIOUS AND ABLE TO SWALLOW: 15-20g fast-acting carbohydrate (glucose tabs, juice, regular soda); Recheck in 15 minutes; Repeat if still <70; Follow with complex carb/protein snack; IF ALTERED OR UNABLE TO SWALLOW: IV ACCESS: D50W 25-50mL (12.5-25g glucose) IV push; May repeat as needed; Follow with D10W infusion if recurrent; NO IV ACCESS: Glucagon 1mg IM/SC/IN; Works within 10-15 minutes; May cause nausea/vomiting; Less effective in depleted glycogen (alcohol, liver disease); SULFONYLUREA-INDUCED: Prolonged monitoring (24-72h) - long half-life!; Octreotide 50-100mcg SC q6-8h (suppresses insulin); D10W infusion preferred over D50 boluses; ADMIT for observation

questions.explanation: Acute Hypoglycemia Management: CONSCIOUS PATIENT: 15-20g fast-acting carbs, recheck in 15 min, repeat if needed. ALTERED/UNABLE TO SWALLOW: IV D50W 25-50mL or Glucagon 1mg IM/SC if no IV access. SULFONYLUREA-INDUCED requires special management: Prolonged monitoring (24-72h) due to long half-life. Octreotide 50-100mcg q6-8h suppresses insulin release. D10W infusion preferred over repeated D50 boluses (which stimulate more insulin release). Admission required.

questions.stem: CT shows a 1.5cm pancreatic mass consistent with insulinoma.
questions.question: How is insulinoma managed?

options.text: Medical management is first-line
Octreotide always improves hypoglycemia
Most insulinomas are malignant

INSULINOMA MANAGEMENT: SURGICAL RESECTION: Treatment of choice - curative in >95%; Enucleation for small tumors; Distal pancreatectomy if body/tail location; Whipple if head location; 90% are benign, solitary; 10% malignant, may have metastases; MEDICAL (if not surgical candidate or awaiting surgery): Diazoxide (inhibits insulin secretion); Start 3-8 mg/kg/day divided; Side effects: edema, hirsutism; Octreotide (somatostatin analog); Inhibits insulin release; May worsen hypoglycemia in some (suppresses glucagon); Frequent small meals; Continuous glucose monitoring; MEN-1 ASSOCIATION: 5-10% of insulinomas; Check for hyperparathyroidism, pituitary tumors; Screen family members

questions.explanation: Insulinoma Management: SURGICAL RESECTION is curative in >95% and is treatment of choice. 90% are benign and solitary. Enucleation for small tumors, pancreatectomy for larger. MEDICAL MANAGEMENT if not surgical candidate: Diazoxide (inhibits insulin secretion), Octreotide (may paradoxically worsen in some patients by suppressing glucagon), frequent meals. MEN-1 ASSOCIATION: 5-10% of insulinomas, screen for hyperparathyroidism and pituitary tumors.

questions.question: What is the complete approach to this patient?

options.text: No need for MEN-1 screening
Image before biochemical confirmation

Complete approach: (1) ACUTE TREATMENT: D50W achieved glucose correction - appropriate initial management; (2) CONFIRM WHIPPLE'S TRIAD: Symptoms (confusion, diaphoresis) + Low glucose (38) + Resolution with treatment - all met; (3) CRITICAL LABS: During hypoglycemia - insulin, C-peptide, proinsulin, BHB, sulfonylurea screen; Her results: High insulin + High C-peptide + Negative SU screen = endogenous hyperinsulinism; (4) LOCALIZATION: CT/MRI pancreas → if negative → EUS → if negative → SACS test; (5) INSULINOMA CONFIRMED: Surgical resection is curative; (6) PREOP MANAGEMENT: Frequent meals, possible diazoxide; (7) SCREEN FOR MEN-1: Calcium, PTH, prolactin; Family history; (8) FOLLOW-UP: Post-op glucose monitoring; Long-term cure expected if benign
Treat with diazoxide long-term without surgery

questions.explanation: Complete Approach: ACUTE: D50W for immediate treatment. CONFIRM WHIPPLE'S TRIAD: All criteria met. CRITICAL LABS during hypoglycemia: Results show endogenous hyperinsulinism (high insulin + high C-peptide + negative sulfonylurea). LOCALIZATION: CT/MRI pancreas, then EUS if needed. TREATMENT: Surgical resection is curative for insulinoma. PREOP: Frequent meals, consider diazoxide. SCREEN MEN-1: Check calcium, PTH, prolactin. PROGNOSIS: >95% cure with surgery if benign (90% of cases).
